title: Visualizing Patch Embeddings
concept: Visualizing the projection weights of a trained ViT reveals filters that learn edge detectors, textures, and color frequencies, similar to early CNN layers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main downside of a very small patch size in ViT?

options.text: It increases the sequence length quadratically, raising computational cost.
It forces the model to ignore position embeddings.

exercises.explain: Correct! Smaller patches create longer sequences, which exponentially increases attention complexity.

cards.title: Behind the curtain
cards.body: What does a transformer see when it looks at a patch embedding? By extracting the learned weights of the projection layer and rendering them as images, we can look behind the curtain and see the model's visual foundation.

cards.title: Projection Filter Visualization
cards.body: Patch embedding visualization displays the weights of the linear projection layer. Because these weights map 2D pixel patches to vectors, we can reshape them back into small 2D images, revealing that they learn to look for specific edges, colors, and textures.

cards.title: A set of colored lenses
cards.body: Imagine you have 768 pairs of glasses. One pair makes vertical lines glow, another highlights red circles, and another highlights grid textures. By wearing all of them at once, you convert a raw scene into a list of details.

cards.title: Gabor-like filters
cards.body: In a trained ViT, the first few principal components of the projection weights resemble Gabor filters (sinusoidal wave patterns) and color opponent filters. This proves transformers naturally learn the same basic vision principles as biology!

cards.title: No local constraint
cards.body: You might think the model was programmed to seek edges. Unlike CNNs which are hardcoded to look locally, ViT is not forced to learn edge detectors. It does so entirely because edges and gradients are the most useful components for understanding images.

left.text:
Tiny size (e.g. 3x3 or 5x5)
Forced local receptive field
Captures simple lines and textures

right.text:
Larger size (e.g. 16x16)
Learns complex internal patterns
Captures edges, gradients, and shapes

exercises.prompt: When we visualize the weights of a trained ViT's linear projection, what do we find?
exercises.explain: Visualizing the projection weights reveals that the transformer learns to detect fundamental visual features like lines and color boundaries.

options.text: Gabor-like filters that detect edges, textures, and color gradients
Exact copy-pastes of the training images

exercises.prompt: True or false: Transformers are mathematically forced to learn edge-detection filters in their projection layer.
exercises.explain: False. Transformers have no spatial inductive bias; they learn edge detectors purely because they are highly predictive for vision tasks.
Reshaping weights lets us view them as visual filters, showing exactly what spatial details trigger each embedding channel.
exercises.sentence: Reshaping the linear projection weights back into 2D matrices allows us to visualize them as visual ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these biological structures operates similarly to the early projection filters in a Vision Transformer?
exercises.explain: Both simple cells in V1 and ViT projection weights specialize in detecting lines, orientations, and basic shapes.

options.text: Simple cells in the primary visual cortex (V1) that react to oriented edges
The optic nerve that transmits raw light signals
The retina that adjusts to low light

exercises.prompt: How does visualizing the projection layer help researchers debug a Vision Transformer?

options.text: If the visualized filters look like random noise, it suggests the model has not trained properly or has failed to extract features.
It tells them the exact final classification accuracy of the validation set.
It automatically rewrites the model code to run faster.
It lets them delete the position embeddings without losing accuracy.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Structured filters are a sign of healthy training; if they remain noisy, it suggests the model hasn't converged. Next: calculating patch sequence lengths!
